rationale: RocksDB is an LSM-tree key-value store where user-facing read/write operations (Get, Put, Seek, MultiGet) execute on application threads, while background maintenance work (compaction, memtable flush, file deletion) runs on dedicated thread pools named 'rocksdb:low', 'rocksdb:high', and 'rocksdb:bottom'. Under CPU oversubscription, these background threads compete with foreground query threads for CPU time, causing tail latency spikes. All RocksDB internal threads share the common prefix 'rocksdb:' (8 bytes), enabling a single prefix match to classify them as background. By deprioritizing all 'rocksdb:*' threads into a low-priority dispatch queue while letting application threads use the kernel's default fast path, the scheduler eliminates interference from compaction and flush work on the latency-critical read/write path. The backup_engine thread is separately named but serves the same background role.

threads.purpose: LOW priority thread pool: executes LSM-tree compaction jobs that merge and reorganize SST files across levels. CPU-intensive and long-running. Scheduled via Env::Schedule with Env::Priority::LOW.

HIGH priority thread pool: executes memtable flush jobs (writing in-memory data to SST files on disk) and obsolete file purge operations. While flush delays can eventually trigger write stalls, these threads do not directly serve user read/write requests.
BOTTOM priority thread pool: executes bottommost-level compaction in universal compaction style. Lowest priority background work, only used when the BOTTOM pool is explicitly configured with threads.

Catch-all for any RocksDB internal thread pool thread. All RocksDB background threads (compaction, flush, bottom-priority, and user-priority pools) share the 'rocksdb:' prefix. This single prefix match captures all internal thread types with minimal BPF overhead.
Backup engine worker threads that process backup items in parallel. Created by BackupEngine for copying SST files during backup operations. Named 'backup_engine' without the 'rocksdb:' prefix.
